title_en: The Lives of the Prophets from Nuh to Musa
title_ar: قصص الأنبياء من نوح إلى موسى
content_en:
Among the most extensive sections of al-Bidayah wan-Nihayah is its account of the lives of the prophets from Nuh (Noah) through Musa (Moses) — the central narrative of divine intervention in human history as recorded in the Quran and authenticated hadiths. Ibn Kathir presents these stories with the methodology of a hadith scholar: verifying the authenticity of each narration, noting where Isra'iliyyat (traditions from Jewish and Christian sources) have been incorporated by earlier historians, and distinguishing between what the Quran explicitly states and what later elaboration has added.

Nuh (Noah) is described in the Quran as the first of the great messengers who called his people to Tawhid after the introduction of idol worship into the world. His preaching spanned — according to the most commonly cited Quranic reference — 950 years: 'And We certainly sent Nuh to his people, and he remained among them a thousand years less fifty years' (Quran 29:14). Despite this extraordinary length of da'wah, his followers were few. When the command came for the Flood, Allah commanded Nuh to build an Ark — an act that his people mocked, as he was building a ship far from any water.

The Flood (al-tufan) is described in the Quran as total — covering the earth and destroying the disbelieving population while Nuh and the believers on the Ark were saved. The scholarly discussion on whether the flood was global or regional has been ongoing; the Quranic text uses language suggesting universality, and the scholarly majority understands it as a universal event for its time, even if questions about its precise geographical scope remain. The story of Nuh's son who refused to board the Ark and drowned — despite being Nuh's biological son — establishes the Quranic principle that divine guidance and salvation are determined by faith, not by blood or family connection.

The subsequent prophets — Ibrahim (Abraham), Ismail (Ishmael), Ishaq (Isaac), Ya'qub (Jacob), and Yusuf (Joseph) — are given extended treatment. The story of Yusuf is the subject of an entire Quranic chapter and is described as 'the best of stories' (Quran 12:3). Ibn Kathir follows the Quranic account meticulously: Yusuf's prophetic dream of the eleven stars, his brothers' jealousy and their casting him into a well, his sale as a slave in Egypt, the attempted seduction by the wife of the Egyptian nobleman (al-Aziz), his imprisonment, his interpretation of dreams, his elevation to the highest position in Egypt, and the eventual reconciliation with his family.

Musa (Moses) receives the most extensive treatment among the pre-Muhammad ﷺ prophets in both the Quran and in Ibn Kathir's historical account. The Quran references Musa in more verses than any other prophet, reflecting his centrality in the divine educational program for humanity. His miraculous birth and preservation from Pharaoh's infanticide decree, his upbringing in Pharaoh's own palace, his killing of the Egyptian and exile in Madyan, his encounter with Allah at the burning bush and the commissioning as prophet, the ten plagues of Egypt, the Exodus across the Sea, the receiving of the Torah at Sinai, and the forty years of wandering in the desert — all receive detailed treatment in Ibn Kathir's account, which cross-references the Quranic verses with the most reliable of the supplementary narrations.
